global_milestone_structure.label: Why Use This?
global_milestone_structure.goal: Explain the real-world problem this concept solves.

global_milestone_structure.label: Concept Analogy
global_milestone_structure.goal: Use a familiar comparison to make the logic intuitive.

global_milestone_structure.label: Getting Started
global_milestone_structure.goal: Show the very first step of the logic in action.

global_milestone_structure.label: Full Execution
global_milestone_structure.goal: Allow the user to see the entire logic run until completion.

computer_architecture.description: Visualize how a computer works internally — memory, CPU, and the fetch-decode-execute cycle

milestones.title: Why Understand Architecture?
milestones.philosophy: Show a laggy computer. Motivation: Every line of code uses hardware resources. Understanding architecture helps write efficient code.

milestones.title: The Chef Analogy
milestones.philosophy: CPU is the Chef, Memory (RAM) is the counter, Hard Drive is the pantry. Fetching from counter is fast; fetching from pantry is slow.

milestones.title: Instruction Fetch
milestones.philosophy: Show RAM with instructions and CPU with an IR register. Animate a value moving from RAM to IR via the 'move' action.

milestones.title: Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle
milestones.philosophy: Show a simple loop (Fetch -> Decode -> Execute). Provide a 'Next Cycle' button. Animate data moving through registers (PC, IR, ACC).

milestones.title: System State
milestones.philosophy: Show the final state of registers and memory after a small program finishes. Summary of the internal data flow.

non_negotiables: PC must increment on every fetch
Memory cells must have explicit addresses (e.g., 0x00, 0x01)
Use 'highlight' on the active register/memory cell

loops.description: Visualize iterative execution with state tracking

milestones.title: Why Loops?
milestones.philosophy: Show a program needing to do 100 similar tasks (e.g., printing 100 lines). Show that writing it line-by-line is painful. Motivation: Efficiency and reuse.

milestones.title: The Treadmill Analogy
milestones.philosophy: Show a treadmill or a lap track. A runner goes in a circle. Each lap, a counter increments. This makes the concept of 'iteration' intuitive before showing code.

milestones.title: The First Iteration
milestones.philosophy: Show the `for` loop initialization and the very first check. Highlight the jump into the loop body. Show the counter `i` becoming 0.

milestones.title: Loop to Completion
milestones.philosophy: Show a countdown or an array processing. Provide a 'Run All' button that iterates through the entire loop until the condition is false. Animate the final exit.

milestones.title: Summary of Loops
milestones.philosophy: A final state screen where the task is complete (e.g. all array elements processed). Compare the start vs end state. Flowchart: Final state showing the path taken and the end node.

non_negotiables: No shape/text goes outside the canvas boundary
Color palette: #1E1E2E (bg), #89B4FA (primary), #A6E3A1 (success), #F38BA8 (error/stop), #CDD6F4 (text)
Every diagram must have a visible text explanation panel below the canvas
Interactive elements must have hover states
Canvas size: fixed 700x400, responsive padding only

control_structures.description: Visualize fundamental control flow mechanisms like conditionals and loops.

milestones.title: Decision Making
milestones.philosophy: Show a smooth program flow suddenly hitting a wall. Motivation: We need logic to handle different cases (e.g., Logged In vs Logged Out).

milestones.title: The Fork in the Road
milestones.philosophy: Show a road that splits into two. One side has a 'True' sign, the other 'False'. Flowchart: An arrow entering a Diamond (Condition) with two exit arrows.

milestones.title: The Condition Check
milestones.philosophy: Show a diamond box with a condition (e.g., x > 5). Highlight it in yellow while it's 'checking'. Animate the flow chose one path based on variable value.

milestones.title: If-Else Execution
milestones.philosophy: Provide a toggle for the variable value. Button: 'Start Flow'. Animate a colored dot (Program Counter) moving through the nodes and arrows to the correct block.

milestones.title: Path Summary
milestones.philosophy: Final state showing which block was executed. Clear 'Result' message on canvas.

non_negotiables: Use Arrows to connect nodes
Diamonds for if-conditions
Colors: #50FA7B for True path, #FF5555 for False path

while_loops.description: Visualize repeated execution based on a continuous condition check.

milestones.title: Handling Uncertainty
milestones.philosophy: Sometimes we don't know how many times to repeat (e.g., wait until user types 'exit'). Motivation: Conditional termination.

milestones.title: The Bouncer Analogy
milestones.philosophy: Show a bouncer at a club door. 'Is the club full?'. If NO, one person enters. The bouncer checks AGAIN for the next person.

milestones.title: First Integration
milestones.philosophy: Show a Diamond (Condition: count < 3). Show an Arrow to a block (Print 'Hello'). Button: 'Step One'. Animate the highlight moving from Diamond -> Block -> Back to Diamond.

milestones.title: Program Flow & Output
milestones.philosophy: Show the Flowchart (Diamond + Body Block + Loopback Arrow). Provide an 'Iterate' button. Every click: 1. Highlight Diamond. 2. Highlight Body. 3. Update 'Console' text at bottom showing 'Hello' repeated. 4. Change Node color to track 'Execution Pointer'.

milestones.title: Termination state
milestones.philosophy: Show the condition becoming FALSE. Flow skips the body block and goes to FINISH node. Final console state shown.

non_negotiables: Must show a 'Console' area for print statements
Body block must have a loopback arrow to the condition
Execution pointer (colored highlight) must be visible

functions.description: Visualize function calls, stack, and parameter passing

milestones.title: Why Functions?
milestones.philosophy: Show 5 identical blocks of code scattered in a main() function. Motivation: Code reuse and modularity (DRY principle: Don't Repeat Yourself).

milestones.title: The Toolbox Analogy
milestones.philosophy: Show a toolbox. Each tool (function) does one specific thing. You 'call' the tool when needed instead of rebuilding it.

milestones.title: Function Call
milestones.philosophy: Show `main()` calling `add(a, b)`. Animate the execution jump from main to the function block.

milestones.title: Parameter Passing & Return
milestones.philosophy: Show values (arguments) flying into function parameters. Animate the calculation. Animate the 'return' value flying back to the caller.

milestones.title: Execution Summary
milestones.philosophy: Show the stack level decreasing as function exits. Summarize the role of inputs and outputs.

non_negotiables: Caller frame colored differently from Callee
Explicit 'Return' button to trigger data flow back

variables.description: Visualize memory, assignment, and data types

milestones.title: Why Variables?
milestones.philosophy: Show a program needing to remember a player's score. Show that without a variable, the data is lost. Motivation: Persistence and memory.

milestones.title: The Box Analogy
milestones.philosophy: Show a labeled box. A value (e.g., '5') is put inside. Clicking the label shows the name, clicking inside shows the value. This makes 'variable name vs value' intuitive.

milestones.title: Declaration and Assignment
milestones.philosophy: Show the code `int x = 10;`. Animate a new box being created in the 'Memory' area and labeled 'x'. Put '10' inside.

milestones.title: Updating Variables
milestones.philosophy: Show a clicker game logic. A variable `count` starts at 0. Button: 'Increment'. Animate the value inside the box updating each click. This shows variables as dynamic storage.

milestones.title: Final Memory State
milestones.philosophy: Show a memory strip with several variables. Verify they all have unique names and current values. Final summary of variable role.

non_negotiables: Variable labels must be distinct from values
Show memory address placeholders (e.g., Box #101)

sorting.description: Visualize comparison-based sorting algorithms step by step

milestones.title: Why Sort?
milestones.philosophy: Show a library with books in random order. Show how long it takes to find 'Harry Potter'. Motivation: Fast searching (binary search requires sorted data).

milestones.title: The Height Lineup Analogy
milestones.philosophy: Show a group of people of different heights. We want to arrange them from shortest to tallest. Compare adjacent people one by one.

milestones.title: The First Swap
milestones.philosophy: Show an array of 6-8 boxes. Compare indices 0 and 1. If 0 > 1, swap them using the 'swap' action. Highlight the compared cells.

milestones.title: Complete Sorting Pass
milestones.philosophy: Provide a 'Step Algorithm' or 'Run Full Pass' button. Clicking it executes swaps until the largest element 'bubbles' to the end. User can repeat until fully sorted.

milestones.title: Sorted State
milestones.philosophy: Show the entire array in green (#50FA7B). Final state verification: check if array is ascending. Success indicator.

non_negotiables: Never show only 1 or 2 elements for sorting; use 6-8.
Use 'swap' action for all element exchanges.
Always highlight compared indices.

milestones.title: Why Sequential Memory?
milestones.philosophy: How does a computer know where a word ends? Motivation: Sequential access and termination markers.

milestones.title: The Train Analogy
milestones.philosophy: Each car is a character. The caboose is the `\0` (null terminator). The train stops when it sees the caboose.

milestones.title: Array Initialization
milestones.philosophy: Show `char s[] = "HI";`. Create an array of 3 boxes. Fill them with 'H', 'I', and '\0'.

milestones.title: String Traversal
milestones.philosophy: Provide a 'Scan' button. Animate a pointer moving through the string until it hits the '\0'. Highlight the terminator.

milestones.title: C-String Memory Map
milestones.philosophy: Final view of how the string looks in RAM. Emphasize that 'Hello' takes 6 bytes, not 5.

non_negotiables: Must show the null character '\0' in red
Show ASCII numeric values below characters
